pros: Large voice and language library (1,000-plus voices, 140-plus languages) at accessible price points
Full commercial usage rights included with generated audio at no extra fee
Free plan allows testing voice quality before subscribing
Voice cloning available for creating custom or branded voices
Low starting price relative to some premium competitors like ElevenLabs
cons: Voice naturalness and emotional expressiveness are generally considered a step behind premium leaders like ElevenLabs for high-production use cases
Small, bootstrapped team may mean slower feature development than well-funded competitors
Word/character allowances on lower tiers can be limiting for heavy content producers
Company details (founders, headquarters) are inconsistently reported across public sources
No major enterprise-grade support or SLA tier commonly advertised compared to larger voice AI vendors

faqs.question: Is Listnr free to use
faqs.answer: Listnr offers a free plan that provides roughly 1,000 words of AI-generated audio without requiring a credit card, with paid plans starting around $9/month for higher usage limits.

faqs.question: Can I use Listnr audio commercially
faqs.answer: Yes, Listnr grants full commercial usage rights over the audio you generate, allowing use in monetized YouTube videos, ads, podcasts, and audiobooks without additional licensing.

faqs.question: Does Listnr support voice cloning
faqs.answer: Yes, Listnr offers AI voice cloning that lets users create a custom synthetic voice model from a sample recording.

faqs.question: How many languages does Listnr support
faqs.answer: Listnr supports more than 140 languages and regional accents across its library of over 1,000 AI voices.

faqs.question: Who founded Listnr
faqs.answer: Listnr was founded in 2020 by Ananay Batra, with Matthias Schedel also credited as a co-founder, and has grown as a largely bootstrapped company.

faqs.question: How does Listnr compare to ElevenLabs
faqs.answer: Listnr is generally positioned as a more affordable, broadly multilingual option, while ElevenLabs is widely regarded as offering more natural and emotionally expressive voice quality for premium production use cases.

use_cases.title: Podcast and YouTube voiceover production
use_cases.description: Independent creators generate narration and voiceovers quickly without hiring voice actors or renting studio time.

use_cases.title: Multilingual marketing localization
use_cases.description: Marketing teams produce localized ad and video voiceovers in dozens of languages from a single script.

use_cases.title: E-learning and course narration
use_cases.description: Course creators convert written training material into narrated audio for online learning modules.

features: Access to a subset of voices
Full commercial rights and priority support

enrichment.short_description: Open-source LLM gateway and Python SDK providing a unified, OpenAI-compatible API across 100-plus LLM providers.
enrichment.starting_price: Free (open source); Enterprise pricing on request
enrichment.seo_title: LiteLLM Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: LiteLLM review 2026: open-source AI gateway unifying 100-plus LLM providers behind one OpenAI-compatible API. Features, pricing, and alternatives compared.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: LiteLLM is an open-source AI gateway and Python SDK, built by BerriAI, that provides a single, unified, OpenAI-compatible interface for calling more than 100 large language model providers, including OpenAI, Anthropic, Google Vertex AI, AWS Bedrock, Azure OpenAI, Cohere, Hugging Face, NVIDIA NIM, and self-hosted inference engines like vLLM. BerriAI was founded in 2023 by Krrish Dholakia and Ishaan Jaffer (who serves as CTO), and the company went through Y Combinator's Winter 2023 (W23) batch, raising roughly $4 million in seed funding co-led by Y Combinator alongside investors including Gravity Fund and Pioneer Fund, with reports of the company reaching around $7 million in annual recurring revenue on a lean team of roughly 10 people, headquartered in San Francisco, California. LiteLLM was built to solve a specific and increasingly common problem in AI engineering: as teams adopt multiple LLM providers for redundancy, cost optimization, or capability differences, each provider's SDK has its own request format, authentication method, and response schema, forcing engineering teams to write and maintain provider-specific integration code throughout their applications. LiteLLM abstracts this away by exposing every provider through the same completion() function call and OpenAI-compatible request and response format, so switching providers, or falling back to a different one during an outage, requires no application code changes. The project ships in two primary forms: a lightweight Python SDK for direct library-level integration inside an application, and a standalone Proxy Server (also marketed as an AI Gateway) that teams can deploy as centralized infrastructure sitting between all of their applications and every LLM provider they use. The proxy server is the more infrastructure-oriented offering, targeted at platform and ML infrastructure teams rather than individual developers, and includes production-grade governance and reliability features: virtual API keys for secure, revocable access control per application or team; per-project, per-user, and per-team budget and rate-limit enforcement; multi-tenant cost tracking and spend attribution across keys, users, teams, and organizations; automatic load balancing and retry/fallback logic across multiple model deployments (so a request can automatically fail over to a backup provider if the primary is down or rate-limited); built-in guardrails for content safety and policy enforcement; and an admin dashboard UI for monitoring usage, spend, and system health. It also integrates with popular LLM observability tools such as Langfuse, Arize Phoenix, Langsmith, and standard OpenTelemetry pipelines, letting teams plug LiteLLM into existing monitoring stacks rather than requiring a proprietary dashboard. LiteLLM's core open-source software, both the SDK and the self-hosted proxy, is completely free to use with no license cost, which has driven substantial adoption: the project has accumulated over 45,000 GitHub stars (with some sources citing over 50,000), more than 240 million Docker image pulls, over one billion API requests served through the proxy in production deployments, and more than 1,000 code contributors, reflecting genuinely broad community and enterprise usage rather than typical open-source vanity metrics. Reported enterprise users include Netflix, Adobe, Stripe, and Lemonade, though the exact scope of each company's deployment is not independently disclosed by LiteLLM. Beyond the free open-source tier, LiteLLM offers an Enterprise plan with custom pricing, adding features aimed at large organizations and regulated environments: enterprise support with SLAs, JWT-based authentication, single sign-on (SSO), audit logging, and self-hosted or fully air-gapped deployment options for organizations that cannot send data to a third-party cloud. LiteLLM's target audience is platform engineering and ML infrastructure teams inside companies building production LLM applications who need centralized governance, cost control, and reliability across multiple model providers, rather than individual hobbyist developers experimenting with a single API. Its competitive set includes other LLM gateway and routing tools such as OpenRouter (a hosted multi-provider API marketplace rather than self-hosted software), Portkey, Helicone, and cloud-native model routing features increasingly built into hyperscaler AI platforms. LiteLLM differentiates itself through being fully open source and self-hostable (versus OpenRouter's hosted-only model), its unusually wide provider coverage (100-plus), and its focus on the specific governance and cost-control needs of platform teams operating LLM infrastructure at scale in production.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: LiteLLM is an open-source AI gateway and Python SDK built by BerriAI, a company founded in 2023 by Krrish Dholakia and Ishaan Jaffer through Y Combinator's W23 batch, headquartered in San Francisco. It provides a single, unified, OpenAI-compatible interface for calling more than 100 LLM providers.

The project solves the problem of provider-specific SDK fragmentation: instead of writing custom integration code for each LLM provider, teams call one consistent completion() interface and can switch or fail over between providers without changing application code.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: LiteLLM ships as a Python SDK for direct integration and as a self-hosted Proxy Server (AI Gateway) offering virtual keys, budgets and rate limits, multi-tenant cost tracking, load balancing, automatic fallback across providers, guardrails, and an admin dashboard.

It integrates with observability tools like Langfuse, Arize Phoenix, Langsmith, and OpenTelemetry, and has scaled to over 45,000 GitHub stars, 240 million-plus Docker pulls, and over one billion production requests served.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: The core open-source SDK and self-hosted proxy are completely free with no licensing cost, including all core gateway features like provider routing, virtual keys, budgets, and load balancing.
An Enterprise plan adds custom pricing with enterprise support, SLAs, JWT authentication, SSO, audit logs, and self-hosted or fully air-gapped deployment options for regulated organizations.

features.title: Unified OpenAI-compatible API
features.description: Call 100-plus LLM providers through one consistent completion() interface, eliminating provider-specific SDK code.

features.title: Provider load balancing and fallback
features.description: Automatically routes requests across multiple model deployments and fails over to backup providers during outages or rate limits.

features.title: Virtual keys and spend tracking
features.description: Issues revocable virtual API keys with per-key, per-user, per-team, and per-project cost tracking and budget enforcement.

features.title: Self-hosted AI Gateway (Proxy Server)
features.description: Deployable as centralized infrastructure between applications and LLM providers, with an admin dashboard for monitoring.

features.title: Guardrails and governance
features.description: Built-in content safety guardrails and policy enforcement for regulated or production-sensitive deployments.

features.title: Observability integrations
features.description: Connects to Langfuse, Arize Phoenix, Langsmith, and OpenTelemetry pipelines for logging and monitoring.

features.title: Enterprise authentication
features.description: JWT authentication and SSO support for enterprise identity and access management requirements.

features.title: Air-gapped deployment
features.description: Enterprise tier supports fully self-hosted, air-gapped deployments for regulated or high-security environments.

pros: Core gateway and SDK are completely free and open source with no feature paywall
Extremely wide provider coverage (100-plus LLMs) through one consistent API
Proven at large scale with over one billion production requests and major enterprise users like Netflix and Adobe
Automatic fallback and load balancing improve reliability across providers
Strong observability integrations fit into existing monitoring stacks rather than forcing a new one
cons: Self-hosting and configuring the proxy for production requires real infrastructure and DevOps effort
Enterprise features (SSO, audit logs, SLAs) require a custom-priced paid plan
Rapidly evolving open-source project can introduce breaking changes between versions
Governance features add complexity that may be unnecessary for small teams using a single provider
Community support model means response times can vary compared to a dedicated commercial vendor

faqs.question: Is LiteLLM free
faqs.answer: Yes, the core open-source SDK and self-hosted proxy server are completely free, including provider routing, virtual keys, budgets, and load balancing. An Enterprise plan with custom pricing adds SSO, audit logs, and SLAs.

faqs.question: Who built LiteLLM
faqs.answer: LiteLLM is built by BerriAI, founded in 2023 by Krrish Dholakia and Ishaan Jaffer, a Y Combinator W23 company headquartered in San Francisco.

faqs.question: How many LLM providers does LiteLLM support
faqs.answer: LiteLLM supports over 100 LLM providers, including OpenAI, Anthropic, Azure, Google Vertex AI, AWS Bedrock, Cohere, Hugging Face, and self-hosted engines like vLLM.

faqs.question: What is the difference between the LiteLLM SDK and the Proxy Server
faqs.answer: The SDK is a Python library for direct in-application integration, while the Proxy Server (AI Gateway) is a standalone, self-hosted service that centralizes routing, cost tracking, and governance across an organization's LLM usage.

faqs.question: Who uses LiteLLM in production
faqs.answer: LiteLLM reports enterprise usage by companies including Netflix, Adobe, Stripe, and Lemonade, alongside more than one billion API requests served in production and over 45,000 GitHub stars.

faqs.question: How does LiteLLM compare to OpenRouter
faqs.answer: LiteLLM is open source and self-hostable, giving teams full control of their infrastructure and data, while OpenRouter is a hosted, multi-provider API marketplace without a self-hosted deployment option.

use_cases.title: Multi-provider LLM infrastructure
use_cases.description: Platform teams centralize access to multiple LLM providers behind one gateway, enabling automatic fallback and cost control across an organization.

use_cases.title: AI cost governance and chargeback
use_cases.description: Organizations track and attribute LLM spend per team, project, or user to manage budgets across many internal applications.
use_cases.audience: Engineering leadership and finance teams at AI-heavy companies

use_cases.title: Provider migration and redundancy
use_cases.description: Teams avoid vendor lock-in by routing through LiteLLM, allowing them to switch or add LLM providers without rewriting application code.
